meta.title: C217 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes S-Class C217 engine — M276, M278, M176, M157, M177, M279. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the last V12 coupé explained. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes S-Class C217 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C217 engine typically costs £4,000–£18,000+ supply only, depending on the engine — the M276 V6 sits at £4,000–£6,500, while the M279 V12 ranges from £12,000–£18,000+. Fitting adds £800–£1,800. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C217 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M176.980 V8 (S 560, 2017–2020) and the M276.824 V6 (S 400/S 450) are the most dependable. The M176 is Mercedes' hot-vee V8 with improved reliability over the M278 — fewer timing-chain and oil-leak issues. The M276 is a well-regarded V6 with a good track record. The M177 in the facelift S 63 is reliable for a 612 hp engine but expensive to maintain.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes S-Class C217?

acceptedAnswer.text: M278 timing chain and camshaft adjuster failure (S 500, pre-facelift), M157/M177 AMG oil leaks and timing chain wear, M279 V12 ignition coil and injector failure, ABC (Active Body Control) suspension failure, and — unrelated to the engine — air suspension issues on non-ABC cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes S-Class C217 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine. The M276 V6 and M176 V8 are genuinely robust. The M278 V8 has known timing-chain issues — budget for inspection. The AMG V8s are reliable for high-performance engines but expensive to maintain. The M279 V12 is a collector's item with collector's bills — treat it as such. The ABC suspension is also a known weak point on high-mileage cars.

mainEntity.name: Which C217 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but pre-facelift M278 V8 (S 500, 2014–2017) needs careful checking for timing chain history. If you're buying one, listen for a rattling noise on cold start — this is the classic early warning sign. The M279 V12 (S 65 AMG) is a specialist engine — if you can afford a £50,000+ car, you can afford the maintenance, but it's not for the faint-hearted.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C217 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain rattle on cold start (M278, M157, M177), oil leaks from the engine bay (AMG V8s), misfires or rough running (M279 V12 coil/injector failure), or ABC suspension warning lights are key warnings. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes S-Class C217 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: The C217 is a premium grand tourer with strong retained values — a £4,000–£6,500 M276 recon against a £20,000 C217 is 20–33% of the car's value, safely below our 70% Mercedes threshold. Even the M278 at £5,000–£8,500 against a £22,000 S 500 is 23–39%. The only exceptions are the AMG models — but if you can afford a £30,000+ S 63 or a £50,000+ S 65, you can afford the maintenance. For the M279 V12, the maths is different — it's a collector's item with collector's bills.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes S-Class C217 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M276 V6 and M176 V8 can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing. The M278 is typically good for 120,000+ miles if the timing chain has been addressed. The AMG V8s (M157, M177) are good for 80,000–120,000 miles if maintained to AMG standards. The M279 V12 is typically good for 80,000+ miles if maintained to AMG standards, but running costs are significantly higher.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C217 and A217?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are the sixth-generation S-Class coupé platform. The C217 is the 2-door coupé (2014–2020, this page) with a fixed roof, while the A217 is the 2-door convertible (2015–2020) with a folding soft-top. They share the same platform, engines, and most components — but the convertible has additional structural reinforcements and a more complex roof mechanism. Costs vary slightly due to the convertible's additional complexity — see the A217 generation page for specifics. The C217 was the last V12-powered Mercedes coupé ever produced.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes S-Class C217 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., S 500, S 560, S 63 AMG) and registration year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes S-Class C217 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C217 coupé — from the M276 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 to the M278 4.7-litre and M176/M177 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8s, and the fire-breathing M157 5.5-litre V8 and M279 6.0-litre V12 in the AMG S 63 and S 65. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the oil-leak and timing-chain issues that define this generation's ownership story. Backed by real UK C217 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes S-Class C217 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C217 coupé specifically — from the entry-level V6 to the range-topping V12, spanning five distinct engine families across pre-facelift and facelift models.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: M276 V6: 80% confidence — a well-regarded twin-turbo V6 with good reliability, though some timing chain and oil leak issues affect higher-mileage examples. M278 V8: 75% confidence — known for timing chain and camshaft adjuster issues, plus oil leaks from the rear main seal. M176 V8: 80% confidence — the newer hot-vee V8 with improved reliability over the M278. M157 V8: 75% confidence — high-performance AMG V8 with good reliability for a 585 hp engine, but expensive when it fails. M177 V8: 75% confidence — AMG's 4.0-litre hot-vee V8 with electric turbochargers; reliable but complex. M279 V12: 70% confidence — the last Mercedes V12, a masterpiece of engineering but eye-wateringly expensive to maintain.

overview.intro: The C217 is the coupé body style of the sixth-generation S-Class, launched in November 2014 as the successor to the CL-Class (C216). Based on the W222 S-Class platform, it features a 2,945 mm wheelbase, pillarless side windows, and a choice of rear-wheel drive or 4MATIC all-wheel drive. The engine range spans the M276 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6, the M278 4.7-litre twin-turbo V8, the M176 and M177 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8s (post-facelift), the M157 5.5-litre twin-turbo V8 (pre-facelift S 63), and the M279 6.0-litre twin-turbo V12 (S 65). A 2017 facelift brought revised styling, new engines, and updated technology. The C217 was the last V12-powered Mercedes coupé ever produced.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W222 (shared with S-Class saloon) • Predecessor: CL-Class (C216) • Successor: Mercedes-AMG SL-Class (R232) • Sibling body style in this generation: A217 Convertible.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C217 S-Class Coupé generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the M278 V8 and M177 AMG V8 as these cars enter enthusiast-owner territory.

items.quote: The 4.0-litre hot-vee V8 M176 is a masterpiece of modern engineering — 469 hp, 700 Nm of torque, and the smoothest V8 Mercedes has made in decades. The 'hot-vee' design places the turbos inside the V of the engine, virtually eliminating lag. It's more reliable than the M278 it replaced, with fewer timing-chain and oil-leak issues. The S 560 is the sweet spot in the C217 range — V8 performance without AMG running costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 refinement and effortless performance without the AMG tax.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 M276 delivers 367 hp with 500 Nm of torque — more than enough for UK roads. It's the cheapest C217 to buy, the cheapest to insure, and the cheapest to replace. The M276 is a well-regarded engine with fewer major failure modes than the V8s. The sweet spot in the C217 range for most UK buyers.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the S-Class coupé experience without the V8 running costs.

items.quote: The 4.7-litre M278 V8 is a great engine — but it comes with known issues: timing chain wear, camshaft adjuster failure, and oil leaks from the rear main seal. The cylinder shut-off system can also cause issues. If you're buying a pre-facelift S 500, budget for timing-chain inspection and rear-main-seal replacement. The M176 in the facelift S 560 is a much safer bet.
items.whoItsFor: buyers willing to accept higher maintenance costs for the pre-facelift V8 experience.

items.quote: The 6.0-litre M279 V12 is the last of its kind — 630 hp, 1,000 Nm of torque, and a sound that no V8 can match. But when it fails, it fails expensively. V12-specific parts are rare and costly, labour is intensive, and the sheer complexity of the twin-turbo 12-cylinder means replacement bills frequently hit five figures. The C217 was the last V12-powered Mercedes coupé ever produced — if you buy one, treat it as a collector's item with a maintenance budget to match.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts with deep pockets who want the last V12 Mercedes coupé.

items.quote: The S 560 is often overlooked in favour of the S 63 AMG, but it offers 469 hp of effortless V8 power with significantly lower running costs. The M176 hot-vee V8 is one of the most refined engines Mercedes has ever built — and with 700 Nm of torque, it's quicker than most drivers will ever need. It's the C217's sleeper pick.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 refinement without the AMG badge tax.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Value — smooth V6, cheaper to run.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain, camshaft adjusters, rear main seal
rows.ownershipVerdict: Highest Risk — great engine, but check timing chain.
Best Petrol — hot-vee V8, refined and reliable.
AMG V8 — fast, but expensive to maintain.
Best AMG — 612 hp, hot-vee, more refined.
Most Expensive — last V12 coupé, collector's item.

economicsRule.title: The C217 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,000–£6,500 M276 recon against a £20,000 C217 sits at 20–33% of the car's value — comfortably safe. Even the M278 at £5,000–£8,500 against a £22,000 S 500 is 23–39% — well within our 70% Mercedes threshold. The C217 is a premium grand tourer with strong retained values; engine replacement is financially viable across the range. The only exceptions are the AMG models — but if you can afford a £30,000+ S 63 or a £50,000+ S 65, you can afford the maintenance. For the M279 V12, the maths is different — it's a collector's item with collector's bills.

problems.title: M278 Timing Chain & Camshaft Adjuster Failure
problems.rootCause: The M278 V8 can suffer from timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster wear. The cylinder shut-off system (Active Cylinder Control) adds complexity. Oil leaks from the rear main seal are also common. A rattling noise on cold start is the classic early warning sign.
problems.recommendation: On any M278-powered C217, listen for a rattling noise on cold start — if you hear it, get the timing chain inspected immediately. A £1,200–£2,200 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £5,000–£8,500 replacement that follows a skipped chain. Also check for oil leaks from the rear main seal — this is a common M278 issue.

cta.label: Compare M278 engine prices for your C217 S 500 →

problems.title: M157/M177 AMG Oil Leaks & Timing Chain Wear
problems.rootCause: The M157 and M177 AMG V8s are high-performance engines that run hot — oil leaks from the valve covers and timing chain covers are common. Timing chain wear can also occur, particularly on higher-mileage examples. A rattling noise after start-up, attributed to a lack of oil pressure in the secondary chain tensioners, has been reported in Service Bulletins.
problems.recommendation: On any S 63 AMG, listen for a rattling noise on cold start — this is a known issue attributed to low oil pressure in the secondary chain tensioners. Mercedes claimed no consequential damage was to be expected, but we recommend preventative inspection. A £1,500–£3,000 repair is far cheaper than the £8,000–£13,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

cta.label: Compare M157/M177 engine prices for your C217 S 63 AMG →

problems.title: M279 V12 Ignition Coil & Injector Failure
problems.rootCause: The 6.0-litre M279 V12 has 12 cylinders, 12 ignition coils, and 12 injectors — all of which can fail. Coil pack failure causes misfires and rough running; injector failure can cause fuel leaks and performance issues. V12-specific parts are expensive and can be hard to source.
problems.recommendation: The M279 V12 is a collector's engine — if you own an S 65, budget for preventative maintenance. A £1,000–£2,000 coil and injector inspection is far cheaper than the £12,000–£18,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. The C217 was the last V12-powered Mercedes coupé ever produced — treat it as the special car it is.

cta.label: Compare M279 engine prices for your C217 S 65 AMG →

problems.title: C217 ABC (Active Body Control) Suspension Failure
problems.affectedModels: All C217 models with ABC suspension (optional on most models, standard on AMG).
problems.rootCause: The C217's Active Body Control (ABC) suspension system uses hydraulic fluid and accumulators to control body roll. The accumulators can fail, the hydraulic lines can leak, and the pump can wear out. This is a complex, expensive system to repair — and a common issue on high-mileage S-Class models.
problems.recommendation: Have the ABC suspension inspected on any C217 — a £300–£600 inspection is far cheaper than the £2,000–£4,000+ replacement that follows a complete system failure. Check for fluid leaks under the car and any warning lights on the dashboard. ABC is a masterpiece of engineering — but it's also a masterpiece with a maintenance budget.

cta.label: Compare C217 ABC suspension repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: C217 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — C217 coupé shares its W222-derived engine bay with the A217 convertible and W222 saloon, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. AMG and V12 costs reflect specialist parts and labour requirements.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are C217 coupé only. A217 Convertible shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, as fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. The C217 was the last V12-powered Mercedes coupé ever produced.
